skill.name: meta-forest-continuous-plot
skill.description: Generate forest plots for meta-analysis of continuous data. Input a CSV file containing study names, means, standard deviations, and sample sizes for experimental and control groups. Output forest plot PNG and data table CSV.
skill.summary: Produce publication-ready forest plots for meta-analysis of continuous outcome data using R or Python scripts.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 76 static analysis findings are false positives upon contextual evaluation. This skill generates forest plots for biomedical meta-analysis. The 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' findings in SKILL.md and README.md are markdown code blocks showing example shell commands and R backtick column naming notation, not actual execution. The 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' findings are scanner pattern-matching errors with no cryptographic references present. The 'os.makedirs' call is legitimate output directory creation. The 'Hardcoded URL' is the source repository link. Previous audit scores this skill 85/100 as 'Production Ready' with all veto gates passing.

low_findings.title: Filesystem write operation in Python script
low_findings.description: os.makedirs() call creates output directory for forest plot files. This is a legitimate operation required to save generated PNG and CSV output. The directory path is derived from user-provided command-line arguments.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Output directory creation is standard and necessary for file generation tools. No unauthorized file access or unusual path manipulation detected.

low_findings.title: R backtick usage for column naming
low_findings.description: R script uses backticks to name a column containing spaces and special characters ('P value (pooled)'). In R, backticks denote column/variable names, not shell command execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: R backtick syntax is standard for column naming with special characters, confirmed by reviewing the R code context. Zero shell execution risk.

low_findings.title: Documentation contains shell command examples
low_findings.description: SKILL.md and README.md contain markdown code blocks showing bash commands for running the scripts. These are documentation examples for user guidance, not automated execution instructions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All shell commands appear inside markdown code blocks as documentation examples. No automated or dynamic command execution is present.

low_findings.title: Scanner false positive for weak cryptographic algorithm
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged lines as 'weak cryptographic algorithm' where no cryptographic references exist. Lines contain statistical terms like SMD (Standardized Mean Difference), CI (Confidence Interval), and column descriptions. These are likely scanner pattern-matching errors.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review confirms zero cryptographic references in any flagged line. Lines contain statistical methodology text and documentation headers.

low_findings.title: Scanner false positive for system reconnaissance
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged data validation checks and JSON evaluation text as 'system reconnaissance'. Lines contain sample size validation (n_e <= 0), audit JSON metadata, and documentation text. None constitute system profiling or reconnaissance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All flagged lines contain standard data validation, audit metadata, or documentation text. No system enumeration, user detection, or environment probing detected.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded GitHub repository URL in documentation
low_findings.description: SKILL.md contains a hardcoded URL pointing to the GitHub source repository. This is the legitimate source attribution link, not a data exfiltration endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: URL points to the skill's own source repository on github.com/aipoch/medical-research-skills. This is standard source attribution, not a suspicious external endpoint.

content.user_title: Generate Forest Plots for Continuous Data Meta-Analysis
content.value_statement: Researchers conducting systematic reviews need to visualize pooled effect sizes across multiple studies. This skill automates forest plot generation from continuous outcome data, producing publication-ready PNG figures and summary statistics tables.
actual_capabilities: Reads CSV data with study names, means, standard deviations, and sample sizes for two groups
Calculates standardized mean difference (SMD) with 95% confidence intervals for each study
Performs random-effects meta-analysis using DerSimonian-Laird method
Computes heterogeneity statistics including I-squared, Tau-squared, and Q-test
Outputs a forest plot as a 300 DPI PNG image with pooled effect diamond
Exports a CSV data table with per-study statistics and pooled results
limitations: Requires R and meta/metafor packages or Python 3.7+ with pandas, numpy, matplotlib
Only supports continuous outcome data (mean and SD), not binary or time-to-event data
Cannot generate subgroup analyses or meta-regression without script modification
Assumes input CSV follows one of two predefined column name formats

use_cases.title: Systematic Review Visualization
use_cases.description: A researcher compiling results from multiple clinical trials on blood pressure reduction can input study statistics and receive a forest plot showing individual and pooled effect sizes.
use_cases.target_user: Medical researcher or epidemiologist conducting a systematic review

use_cases.title: Meta-Analysis for Publication
use_cases.description: An academic author preparing a manuscript for a peer-reviewed journal needs standardized forest plots that meet publication formatting requirements, including heterogeneity statistics.
use_cases.target_user: Academic author preparing a meta-analysis manuscript

use_cases.title: Evidence Synthesis for Guidelines
use_cases.description: A guideline development panel member synthesizing evidence on treatment efficacy across multiple randomized controlled trials to inform clinical practice recommendations.
use_cases.target_user: Clinical guideline developer or evidence synthesis specialist

prompt_templates.title: Basic forest plot generation
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a forest plot for meta-analysis of continuous data using the file at [path/to/data.csv]. The CSV has columns: study, group1_sample_size, group1_Mean, group1_SD, group2_sample_size, group2_Mean, group2_SD.
prompt_templates.scenario: First-time user running a standard analysis with properly formatted input

prompt_templates.title: Specify outcome name and output directory
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the meta-analysis forest plot skill using data from [path/to/data.csv]. The outcome measure is 'Quality of Life'. Save the output files to [path/to/output/].
prompt_templates.scenario: User needs to label the outcome and control where files are saved

prompt_templates.title: Using alternative CSV column format
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a forest plot from the CSV at [path/to/data.csv]. The file uses column headers: Study, Mean.e, SD.e, Total.e, Mean.c, SD.c, Total.c. The outcome is 'Pain Score'.
prompt_templates.scenario: User has data in the alternative column format compatible with both R and Python scripts

prompt_templates.title: Fallback when R is unavailable
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a forest plot using the Python fallback script. Input data is at [path/to/data.csv] with columns study, group1_sample_size, group1_Mean, group1_SD, group2_sample_size, group2_Mean, group2_SD. Outcome name is 'Anxiety Score'.
prompt_templates.scenario: User environment does not have R installed; must use the Python implementation

output_examples.input: User provides a CSV with 5 studies measuring quality of life scores between intervention and control groups
output: A PNG forest plot titled 'Forest map - Quality of Life' showing SMD point estimates and 95% CI for each study, with a pooled diamond at the bottom
Summary statistics: SMD = -0.12 [-0.28; 0.03], P = 0.1241, I-squared = 34% indicating moderate heterogeneity

output_examples.input: User runs the script on a dataset of 4 blood pressure trials with outcome name 'Systolic BP'
output: A forest plot with four study rows and a pooled effect diamond, showing a significant reduction in systolic blood pressure
A CSV data table containing per-study means, SDs, sample sizes, SMD values with confidence intervals, and random-effects weights

best_practices: Validate your input CSV has all required columns and at least 2 studies before running the analysis to avoid script errors.
Use absolute file paths for the CSV input and output directory to ensure the R or Python script can locate files correctly.
Check that the required R packages (meta, metafor, grid, stringr) are installed if using the R script, or use the Python fallback if R is unavailable.
anti_patterns: Do not modify the R or Python script files to change analysis methods unless you understand the statistical implications of the changes.
Do not use this skill for binary outcome data (event counts) or individual patient data, as it only supports aggregate continuous data.
Do not skip the input validation step, as missing required columns or invalid numeric values will cause script failures.

faq.question: What input data format does this skill require?
faq.answer: The skill accepts CSV files with either of two column formats: Format 1 uses study, group1_sample_size, group1_Mean, group1_SD, group2_sample_size, group2_Mean, group2_SD. Format 2 uses Study, Mean.e, SD.e, Total.e, Mean.c, SD.c, Total.c.

faq.question: What R packages are needed for the R script?
faq.answer: The R script requires the meta, metafor, grid, and stringr packages. Install them with install.packages(c('meta', 'metafor', 'grid', 'stringr')).

faq.question: Can I use the Python script instead of R?
faq.answer: Yes, the Python script at scripts/forest_continuous.py serves as a fallback. It requires Python 3.7+ with pandas, numpy, and matplotlib installed.

faq.question: What statistical method does this skill use?
faq.answer: The skill uses the DerSimonian-Laird random-effects model to calculate pooled standardized mean difference (SMD). It also reports fixed-effects results and heterogeneity statistics including I-squared and Q-test.

faq.question: What output files does the skill generate?
faq.answer: It generates two files: a PNG forest plot image at 300 DPI and a CSV data table containing per-study statistics, SMD values, confidence intervals, weights, and the pooled result row.

faq.question: How many studies are needed for the analysis?
faq.answer: The skill requires at least 2 studies to conduct a meta-analysis. If fewer than 2 valid study rows are detected, the script will return an error message.
